coredata.dc:title: Interfacial interactions of submicron plastics with carbon dots: Insights into the interface properties of microplastic weathering
coredata.prism:copyright: © 2025 Elsevier Ltd. All rights are reserved, including those for text and data mining, AI training, and similar technologies.
coredata.prism:publisher: Elsevier Ltd. All rights are reserved, including those for text and data mining, AI training, and similar technologies.
coredata.dc:creator: Liu, Chao
Jiao, Yuan
Yang, Chunfan
Li, Bo
Li, Wenjun
Qian, Tianwei
Liu, Xiaona
coredata.dc:description: The interfacial properties and environmental behavior of microplastics (MPs) will change with weathering. A new idea to study the interfacial properties of MPs is provided based on fluorescence response and light scattering changes. Submicron microspheres (PS-AA) obtained by soap-free emulsion polymerization have a well-defined composition and clean surface with carboxyl groups. The interfacial properties of PS-AA changed after Fenton and UV aging, and the sharp edges became blurred. Information on the interfacial interactions of leaf-derived carbon dots (R-CDs) and citrate carbon dots (B-CDs) with aged PS-AA was obtained by recording fluorescence and scattering changes. R-CDs can fluorescently respond to carrying contaminants on aged PS-AA, and their correlation increases with the degree of aging (R2=0.8388). The scattering peak of PS-AA decreased after aging, and the change in scattering/fluorescence ratios with concentration had a good linear relationship under the coexistence of B-CDs (R2=0.9983). Aging of PS-AA increases the contamination-carrying capacity and decreases the optical properties, which may be attributed to the increased oxygen-containing functional groups, ring opening of substituted benzene, and shell decomposition. The response mechanism of carbon dots (CDs), the aging process of PS-AA, and the interfacial behavior were further explained based on the density functional theory (DFT). This study reveals the changes in interfacial properties of submicron plastics with the aging process based on fluorescence response and scattering changes.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Microplastics
Carbon dots
Weathering
Interfacial interaction
Fluorescence response
Elastic scattering

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Synthesis of submicron plastics and carbon dots
Density functional calculations and data analysis
Interfacial changes of PS-AA during aging process
Changes in functional groups of PS-AA during the aging process
Fluorescence response of R-CDs with ps-aa at different degrees of aging
Changes in scattering intensity of PS-AA coexisting with B-CDs at different aging degrees

xocs:cp-notices.xocs:cp-notice: © 2025 Elsevier Ltd. All rights are reserved, including those for text and data mining, AI training, and similar technologies.

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: The characteristics and optical properties of R-CDs and B-CDs. (a-b, g-h) TEM; (c) XRD; (d, i) Excitation and emission spectra; (e) Fluorescence lifetime; (f, j) FTIR.
Surface morphology (a–g) and XPS (h–k) analysis of submicron plastics before and after aging.
FTIR (a–c) and 2D-COS (d–i) analysis of submicron plastics under different aging conditions.
Fluorescence response of R-CDs with submicron plastics after NF and UV aging. (a-p) Fluorescence spectral data; (q, r) Linear Fitting for fluorescence intensity.

The changes of scattering light under coexistence conditions of different aged submicron plastics with B-CDs. (a) Scattering spectra when only PS-AA is present. (b) Scattering spectra of PS-AA in coexistence with B-CDs. (c-f) Changes in scattering peaks in the coexistence of B-CD and PS-AA with different degrees of aging.

The DFT computational simulation of CDs and PS-AA. (a–d) The electrostatic potentials. (e) The response mechanism of R-CDs. (f–i) Simulation of changes in HOMO-LUMO gaps of PS-AA with the aging process. (j, k) Analysis of the aging process of PS-AA based on Fukui functions (Green and blue colors represent positive and negative values, respectively, ISO 0.002 a.u).
2D−COS data on the sign of each cross-peak in synchronous (Φ) and asynchronous (Ψ, in the brackets) maps.

head.ce:title: Interfacial interactions of submicron plastics with carbon dots: Insights into the interface properties of microplastic weathering

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: dWanli Energy Technology Development Co., Ltd, Zhejiang Wanli University, Ningbo, 315100, China

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: The interfacial properties and environmental behavior of microplastics (MPs) will change with weathering. A new idea to study the interfacial properties of MPs is provided based on fluorescence response and light scattering changes. Submicron microspheres (PS-AA) obtained by soap-free emulsion polymerization have a well-defined composition and clean surface with carboxyl groups. The interfacial properties of PS-AA changed after Fenton and UV aging, and the sharp edges became blurred. Information on the interfacial interactions of leaf-derived carbon dots (R-CDs) and citrate carbon dots (B-CDs) with aged PS-AA was obtained by recording fluorescence and scattering changes. R-CDs can fluorescently respond to carrying contaminants on aged PS-AA, and their correlation increases with the degree of aging (R2=0.8388). The scattering peak of PS-AA decreased after aging, and the change in scattering/fluorescence ratios with concentration had a good linear relationship under the coexistence of B-CDs (R2=0.9983). Aging of PS-AA increases the contamination-carrying capacity and decreases the optical properties, which may be attributed to the increased oxygen-containing functional groups, ring opening of substituted benzene, and shell decomposition. The response mechanism of carbon dots (CDs), the aging process of PS-AA, and the interfacial behavior were further explained based on the density functional theory (DFT). This study reveals the changes in interfacial properties of submicron plastics with the aging process based on fluorescence response and scattering changes.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: Data will be made available on request.

ce:section.ce:para: MPs are defined as plastic particles with a particle size of less than 5 mm in the environment (Thompson et al., 2024), which originate from the improper use of plastic products. MPs are weathered and degraded into smaller submicron/nano plastics in the environment, which pose a greater risk. With this process, the interfacial properties of MPs are changed differently and can lead to different environmental behaviors as well as toxic effects (Liu et al., 2024c; Su et al., 2022). Environmental detection of MPs has been a major challenge, especially for submicron or nano plastics. Conventional spectroscopy and mass spectrometry are limited by particle size or minimum sample mass (Okoffo et al., 2022; Phan et al., 2022), and it is difficult to evaluate differences in weathering, interfacial properties, and carrying contamination of MPs.

MPs are susceptible to weathering and interacting with other substances in the environment. After weathering, bond breaking, oxidation, dehydrogenation, and the generation of oxygen-containing functional groups (C=O and -OH) on the surface are able to form hydrogen bonds with water molecules, to improve the hydrophilicity of MPs. Moreover, the aging (UV-365 nm±50 nm, 24 h) of MPs increases the TOC content of the solution (Liu et al., 2019b), and the increase in surface O/C also enhances the adsorption capacity of MPs (Liu et al., 2019). Weathered MPs can carry multiple contaminants (Nurain et al., 2024; Zhang et al., 2024b) and cause changes in interfacial behavior (Huang et al., 2024). Li et al. (2024) reported that the “microplastic-electric field” coupling of aging MPs affects soil aggregation. MPs have the capacity to adsorb soil water-soluble soil metabolites (Thymine, aminocaproic acid, betaine, L-isoleucine, etc.) through direct adsorption and macromolecular bridging, forming eco-corona (Yao et al., 2023). In addition, this interfacial change also affects the internalization of MPs into cells (Wieland et al., 2024). Therefore, carrier effects can affect the detection and assessment of MPs in the environment.

The surface properties of MPs in the environment are critical for their behavior prediction, detection, and removal. The hydrophobic hydrocarbon chains on the surface of MPs were utilized to achieve surfactant flotation for MPs removal by Jiang et al. (2023). Wu et al. (2024) achieved semi-quantitative detection of carboxylated polystyrene MPs using a cationic fluorescent probe. The mechanism is based on fluorescence enhancement caused by intramolecular rotational restriction through electrostatic and hydrophobic interactions. Mou et al. (2024) evaluated MPs in bottled water using Mie scattering and noted that this method is independent of the type and size of MPs. It is worth noting that the aging-induced changes in the structure of MPs (C=C, C=O, etc.) affect their surface and optical properties, making the actual detection process even more complex (Wang et al., 2023; Monteleone et al., 2021). Recently, Liu et al. (2024b) based on the polarized light scattering technique combined with the neural network can analyze the polarization characteristics of MPs with different weathering degrees and types. Therefore, for the detection and assessment of MPs, it is crucial to understand the changes in interfacial properties during weathering.

As technology has evolved, researchers have begun to explore the use of fluorescence techniques to analyze the properties of MPs. After combining fluorescent nanoparticles with MPs by hot-melting, the fluorescence of the nanoparticles is red-shifted by the polarity of the plastic (Zhang et al., 2022). This is similar to CDs, in which the interaction of CDs with contaminants alters the initial electron transition and charge transfer (Long et al., 2021; Jiao et al., 2023), resulting in fluorescence enhancement or quenching. Polystyrene MPs has been widely reported in environmental monitoring (Yang et al., 2023) and is often used to evaluate the environmental behavior and toxic effects of submicron and nano plastics, which typically have carboxylated surface groups (Lou et al., 2022; Liu et al., 2023; Dong et al., 2023). CDs are generally smaller in particle size and richer in functional groups than MPs. Therefore, it is possible for CDs to alter optical behavior under the influence of functional groups or carrying contaminants on MPs.

In this study, based on leaf-derived red emission and citric acid blue emission carbon dots, the changes in the interfacial behavior of submicron microplastics during aging were investigated, including fluorescence and scattered light. Red and blue emission CDs have different color space and elemental compositions, which provides more precise experimental data to study the complex interactions between MPs and CDs. Changes in the fluorescence properties and scattering intensity can be used as an indication of the weathering degree and surface properties of MPs after aging. This study provides new insights into understanding the surface weathering characteristics of MPs and their effects on the adsorption of pollutants in the environment. It is expected to provide theoretical support for the evaluation of MPs detection and aging process methods.

Styrene, KBr, and FeSO4 were supplied by Meryer Chemical Technology Co., Ltd (Shanghai, China). Acrylic acid and NaHCO3 were purchased from Adamas Reagent Co., Ltd (Switzerland). K2S2O8, H2O2, NaOH, KCl, and Ethanol absolute were obtained from Sinopharm Chemical Reagent Co., Ltd (Shanghai, China). H2SO4 (0.5 mol/L) were supplied by Shanghai Anpel Co., Ltd (Shanghai, China). 3500 Da Dialysis bag was purchased from G-Clone Co., Ltd (Beijing, China). Citric acid and Urea were purchased from Macklin Co., Ltd (Shanghai, China).

ce:section.ce:section-title: Synthesis of submicron plastics and carbon dots
ce:section.ce:para: Submicron polystyrene microspheres were synthesized by soap-free emulsion polymerization. Dissolve 0.05 g NaHCO3 and 0.017 g K2S2O8 in 25 mL deionized water and place in a three-necked flask. When the temperature reached 70°C, 1.5 mL 99% styrene and 0.25 mL 98% acrylic acid (The molar ratio is 4.2:1) were added and the reaction was protected by nitrogen for 7 h (Liu et al., 2024). It was cooled naturally to room temperature and dialyzed for 3 d using dialysis bags.

R-CDs were obtained by the hot solvent method. The reaction was accomplished under high temperature and pressure using leaf extracts as precursors and ethanol as organic solvents. The leaves of the campus shrub (Buxus megistophylla H. Lév.) were clipped and soaked using ethanol for 24 hours. The extract was rotary evaporated and freeze-dried to obtain the powder (the main component was chlorophyll). The 0.15 g of chlorophyll was dissolved in 30 mL of ethanol and reacted at 200°C for 8 h using a 50 mL polyvinylidene fluoride reactor. The solution was cooled to room temperature and then passed through a 0.22 um needle filter membrane and dialyzed for 3 d using a dialysis bag.

B-CDs were prepared by hydrothermal synthesis. 1 g citric acid and 0.95 g urea were weighed and dissolved in 25 mL of deionized water and reacted using a 50 mL reactor at 200°C for 8 h. After the cool down, it was passed through a 0.22 um filter membrane and dialyzed using a dialysis bag for 3 d.

Finally, the solid mass is recorded with an analytical balance (0.29±0.09 mg/mL for R-CDs and 0.68±0.02 mg/mL for B-CDs, the accuracy is 0.1 mg). All materials are stored away from light at room temperature.

1 mL of microspheres were added to a 30 mL sample vial. Then, 0.1 mL of 30% H2O2 and 6.47 mL of H2O were added, followed by 0.1 mL of 200 mM Fe2+ (Liu et al 2019; Lang et al., 2020). The pH of H2O2 and Fe2+ was pre-adjusted to 4, and the polystyrene microspheres were pre-treated by dialysis in deionized water at pH 4. The solutions were treated under a constant temperature shaker for 0 d, 1 d, 4 d, 7 d, and 10 d (NF treatment, Fenton). After aging, dialyze with pH 4 deionized water for 1 day, and then dialyze with deionized water for 2 days. Remove other components from the solution and store in the dark.

Similarly, 1 mL of microspheres were added to a 30 mL sample vial, and 6.67 mL of H2O were added. The samples were exposed to UV light (254 nm and 365 nm, 6–8 W) for 0 d, 10 d, 20 d, and 30 d (UV treatment). Water was supplemented during the period. After aging, all samples were dialyzed with deionized water for 3 d, and stored away from light. In addition, samples with only H2O2 added were set as controls (NH treatment, pH=4).

During the dialysis period, the dialysate was changed every 24 h (1000 mL, volume ratio of dialysate to sample more than 100:1 every time). All drying programs were set at 50°C. All experiments were repeated 3 times and the solid mass was recorded (Table S1).

The interfacial behavior of CDs and MPs was analyzed when coexisting in aqueous using 3 mL of CDs as the initial system. Information on fluorescence and scattered light was obtained using a fluorescence spectrophotometer (Shimadzu-RF6000 at 5 nm slit). The structural features of CDs and MPs were observed by transmission electron microscopy (TEM, JEOL JEM-F200) and X-ray diffraction (XRD, Panalytical-Aeris). The fluorescence lifetimes of CDs were determined by transient steady-state fluorescence spectroscopy (FLS980-STM). Changes in surface functional groups were determined by Fourier transform infrared spectroscopy (FTIR, PerkinElmer) and analyzed by 2D-COS using 2Dshige software (2Dshige (c) Shigeaki Morita, Kwansei-Gakuin University, 2004–2005). The samples were scanned by X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS, Thermo Scientific ESCALAB Xi+) to determine surface elements and valence states. Scanning electron microscopy (SEM-EDS, Hitachi-SU8010) was used to observe the morphological changes of the samples before and after treatment. The potential and hydrodynamic diameter of PS-AA were recorded by a zeta potential meter (Nanobrook 90Plus PALS). Micromeritics TriStar II 3020 Multiport Surface Area and Porosity Analyzer was used to analyze the change in BET surface area before and after aging.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Density functional calculations and data analysis
ce:section.ce:para: The surface electrostatic potential and electron energy changes of simulated CDs and PS-AA were calculated using Gaussian 16 (Revision C.02, DFT functional/basis set: B3LYP/6–31G*). The results of the calculations were processed and plotted using Multiwfn 3.8 (Lu et al., 2012; Lu, 2024) and VMD 1.9.3 (Humphrey et al., 1996). The detailed process is described in Text 1.

Data were analyzed using SPSS software with one-way ANOVA and LSD post hoc multiple tests. Differences were considered significant for p<0.05. The particle size was measured using Nano Measurer 1.2 software.

Fluorescent R-CDs were synthesized based on the leaf extracts of campus shrubs. Organic solvents were removed from R-CDs by dialysis to exclude experimental interference. According to the results of TEM and XRD (Fig. 1 a-c), the synthesized R-CDs have an average particle size of 3.08 nm with a distinct crystalline structure of graphitic carbon corresponding to 28.42° (002) and 40.50° (100) crystal planes of the graphitic structure, respectively. The broad peak at around 22° indicates the presence of amorphous carbon structure. The fluorescence properties of R-CDs are shown in Fig. 1d. R-CDs have the same fluorescence emission under a variety of excitation conditions, with the optimal excitation/emission wavelengths of 415/676 nm in the visible range. The excitation and emission properties of the R-CDs are essentially unchanged in the different solvents, with the difference that the excitation signal is enhanced at lower wavelengths (375 nm). The fluorescence lifetime (Fig. 1e) of the R-CDs was further determined by transient steady-state fluorescence spectroscopy (1.13 ns), which reflects the duration of fluorescence emitted upon return to the ground state after excitation.

The infrared spectral information of R-CDs is shown in Fig. 1f. The absorption peaks near 3436 cm-1 are -OH and -NH. The absorption peaks at 2926 and 2854 cm-1 respond to the antisymmetric and symmetric stretching vibrations of methylene (-CH2-), and 1464 cm-1 corresponds to the bending vibration. The absorption peaks at 1633 and 1067 cm-1 are attributed to C=O and C-O bonds. While 720 cm-1 is [CH2]n (n≥4), the strength of its absorption peak reflects the crystalline nature of the R-CDs.

B-CDs with stronger fluorescence intensity were synthesized by citric acid and urea. The average particle size of B-CDs is 4.03 nm, and the fluorescence lifetime is 4.81 nm (Fig. S1). The TEM results did not observe significant interplanar spacing (Fig. 1g-h), which may be due to the influence of nitrogen on the formation of graphene crystal structure. Element doping can introduce more nitrogen-containing functional groups, change the polarity and surface charge, and enhance fluorescence performance. The optimum excitation of B-CDs is 360 nm and the optimum emission is 432 nm (Fig. 1i). The FTIR of which is shown in Fig. 1j. Its absorption peaks at around 3430 cm-1 are O-H. The absorption peaks at about 3230 cm-1 are attributed to functional groups related to N-H. The absorption peaks at about 2932 and 2860 cm-1 are covered. Its absorption peaks at 2932 and 2860 cm-1 associated with C-H bonds are masked. 1668, 1572, 1189, and 639 cm-1 have distinct absorption peaks all of which are characteristic peaks of citric acid and urea CONH, C=N, C-N, C-O-C, [CH2]n.

The structure of the R-CDs and B-CDs has stable properties, with less solvent dependence of excitation and emission, and better light absorption and conversion efficiencies. In addition, the R-CDs have a large Stokes shift long wavelength emission. It has good resistance to background fluorescence. B-CDs have more nitrogen-containing functional groups better water solubility, smaller Stokes shift, higher fluorescence intensity, and longer fluorescence lifetime.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Interfacial changes of PS-AA during aging process
ce:section.ce:para: Fig. 2 a–g shows the surface morphology of PS-AA after different aging treatments. The different treatments have a small effect on the particle size of submicron plastics. The primary submicron plastic surface is not smooth and flat, but rather riddled with cracks and hollows. This means that the sub-micron plastic microspheres have a very large specific surface area and are highly susceptible to the adsorption of other pollutants in the environment. The particle size after aging increased from the initial 280.23 to 287.86 nm (the average value, Table S1). Increase in PS-AA particle size due to breakage of chemical bonds such as C=C on the benzene ring or C-C on the main chain (Ding et al., 2020). According to the theory of oligomer micelle nucleation, the shell region is generated after polymerization nucleation, with a thickness of about 10–40 nm (Chen et al., 1991), and the aging process affected by external factors mainly occurs in this part.

The surface of the aged PS-AA was changed. The most pronounced aging among the three treatments was the NF treatment, followed by UV, while NH had the weakest aging effect. After aging, the sharpness of the surface protrusions decreases and becomes more irregular. The initially sharp edges became blurred, indicating that smaller crack structures were generated on the surface of the microspheres, which implies that aged MPs have a greater specific surface area and adsorption capacity, with greater environmental risk and ecotoxicity.

According to the results of the EDS mapping, the O/C atomic ratio of the MPs surface showed an increasing trend after aging (Fig. S2, Table S2). The O/C ratio after NF treatment was 0.0210, followed by UV treatment at 0.0077, while NH treatment showed insignificant changes. It is noteworthy that the NF treatment induced deposition of Fe on the surface of the MPs, which remained after the dialysis treatment (0.89 atomic%). This may be due to the increase in the specific surface area of the aged MPs and the increase in the number of adsorption sites (Fig. S3), thus enhancing the adsorption and deposition of Fe.

According to the results of XPS characterization (Fig. 2h–k), the intensity of the carbon peaks decreased after aging, while the binding energy did not shift significantly. This is because the copolymerized monomer contains O-C=O, so it is difficult to recognize the change of functional groups by the change of binding energy.

MPs are inevitably aged in the environment, increasing specific surface area and adsorption capacity. This is highly likely to adsorb other pollutants, leading to greater environmental hazards. Zhang et al. (2024) showed that the surface properties (roughness and oxygen-containing functional groups) of aged polystyrene had a high affinity for Pb2+ and nanozero-valent iron, and the formation of an encapsulated film on the surface of nanozero-valent iron accelerated the aging of MPs.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Changes in functional groups of PS-AA during the aging process
ce:section.ce:para: The FTIR of different aged PS-AA is shown in Fig. 3 a–c. The peaks at 3435 and 1375 cm-1 are attributed to -OH. The absorption peaks near 3000 cm-1 represent C-H on the carbon chain and benzene ring, respectively. The characteristic peak of the copolymerized monomer (O-C=O) is observed at 1712 cm-1, and 1635 cm-1 is attributed to C=O. 1100 cm-1 is C-O. The absorption peak at 2660 and 1712–1635 cm-1 is a sign of aldehyde group generation (Rabek et al., 1974), which may be related to the ring opening of the benzene ring. The functional group changes in the range of 1712–1635 cm-1 were studied through area integration (Table S3). It was observed that the integral area increased after aging, with the UV aging having the largest value, followed by NF, and NH having the smallest. However, by analyzing the ratio with methylene, it can be found that NF is the largest. The infrared absorption changes before and after ring-opening were simulated by DFT calculations, and a significant change in the vibration of the benzene ring skeleton can be observed (Fig. S4). Due to the differences between the simulation results and the actual results, only the relative position and intensity of peaks are compared.

The changes in the infrared spectra of PS-AA under different aging treatments were basically the same, showing enhanced absorption peaks of oxygen-containing functional groups. This change is most clearly illustrated by the -OH and C=O peaks at 1375 and 1635 cm-1. Multiple aging increases the intensity of the absorption peaks of oxygen-containing functional groups as the degree of aging increases. The change in oxygenated functional groups with aging was further quantified by the peak intensity of oxygenated functional groups relative to the methylene peak (Liu et al., 2019), as shown in Table S3. The carbonyl/hydroxyl indices of NF (from 0.86/0.50 to 2.36/1.98) and NH (from 0.86/0.50 to 1.69/0.96) aged PS-AA increased, but both exhibited a maximum followed by a decrease at 1 days of aging. The ratio of carbonyls to methylene groups may be affected by processes such as benzene ring opening (Fig. S4). For UV aging, the hydroxyl index increases gradually with aging time but at a lower degree. Conversely, the carbonyl index decreases instead. The surface UV aging process is distinct from the NF and NH aging process, as the secondary derivatives generated result in a reduction in the number of carbonyls (Mao et al., 2020; Wang et al., 2024). Moreover, the changes in carbonyl and hydroxyl groups after aging are also influenced by humidity. Wang et al. (2023b) pointed out in their study that 90% humidity promotes the formation of hydroxyl group while inhibiting the formation of the carbonyl group.

Further analysis using 2D-COS (Table 1 ) revealed that the three aging treatments resulted in similar changes (Lasch et al., 2019; Noda et al., 2004). Synchronous spectroscopy indicates the extent to which PS-AA is affected by aging. And asynchronous spectroscopy reflects changes in the phase anisotropy of the functional groups, reflecting partial changes. Among them, NF aging has the largest range and intensity of changes to the FTIR spectrum. This result is consistent with the results of the carbonyl index and O/C ratio. UV aging affects functional groups in a different range relative to NH aging, while the intensity is higher, which is consistent with the results above.

In addition, the effects of the three aging processes on the functional groups of PS-AA are different. The order in which the functional groups change during NF aging is: 1635 (C=O)>1375 (C-OH)>1300 (C-C),1100 (C-O). NF treatments were aged for 1, 4, 7, and 10 days, and C=O was the first to be produced, with the highest amount corresponding to the change in carbonyl index. The eventual change in the C-C bond means that the first chemical bond to be affected by aging is the one in the branched chain and the final result of aging leads to the breakage of the main C-C bond. The order of functional group changes due to NH treatment is 1635 (C=O)>1300 (C-C), 1100 (C-O). And unlike the results of the NF/NH treatment, the UV aging process is a combination of UV radiation and oxygen (Mao et al., 2020). The functional group changes due to UV aging are: 1300 (C-C)>1375 (C-OH)>1100 (C-O),1635 (C=O). The C-C bond absorbs at wavelengths between 195 nm and 230–250 nm, whereas C-OH absorbs at 230 nm, so the C-C and C-OH bond is most likely to change under the excitation of UV radiation. The sequence of functional group changes is mainly H-substitution oxidation and chain breaking.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Fluorescence response of R-CDs with ps-aa at different degrees of aging
ce:section.ce:para: The mutual interfacial interaction between the three aged PS-AA and the R-CDs led to changes in fluorescence emission, with the results shown in Figs. 4 and S5. Among them, NF aging has the greatest effect on the fluorescence intensity of R-CDs. As the concentration of NF-aged PS-AA increased, the fluorescence intensity of the solution showed an obvious decreasing trend. UV aging of PS-AA results in slightly enhanced fluorescence of the solution. NH aging had no significant effect, similar to the pristine PS-AA. Therefore, the effects of NF and UV aging on the fluorescence of R-CDs were further analyzed.

The influence of NF-aged PS-AA on the fluorescence of R-CDs is shown in Fig. 4a–h. The inhibition of fluorescence emission from the PS-AA on the R-CDs was enhanced with increasing aging. In addition, the fluorescence intensity decreased with increasing PS-AA concentration, and the linear correlation increased with increasing aging. The higher the degree of aging, the higher the value of R2 (Fig. 4q). Based on this, the analysis of the effect of aging degree on fluorescence intensity at the same concentration is shown in Fig. S6. The fluorescence intensity decreases with the increase of aging degree at a certain concentration, and all of them have a strong linear relationship. The correlation coefficient is enhanced with increasing concentration.

Fluorescence quenching occurred due to the strong charge transfer ability of Fe with active outer electrons, which inhibits the electron transition process of R-CDs. However, all Fe2+/Fe3+ in the solution of PS-AA treated by dialysis are microplastic carrying contaminants. Numerous studies have demonstrated that MPs in the environment are aged over time. Aging MPs have smaller particle size, larger specific surface area, more adsorption sites, and are able to adsorb a variety of environmental pollutants such as heavy metals (Li et al., 2021) and organics (Liu et al., 2020), thus causing greater environmental hazards. In this experiment, the specific surface area of PS-AA doubled after NF aging (from 15.71 to 36.56 m2/g). As the roughness and oxygen-containing functional groups of PS-AA increased with aging (Fig. S3), this resulted in more negative charges on the surface and increased adsorption as well as deposition of iron, which enhanced the quenching of R-CDs. A significant fluorescence quenching can be observed by the quenching experiment of Fe2+ individually (Fig. S5). By comparison, the aging effect was able to significantly increase the adsorption and deposition of Fe on the PS-AA. Therefore, R-CDs have great potential value for the environmental risk assessment and the study of carrier pollutants of MPs.

The influence of PS-AA after UV aging on the fluorescence emission of R-CDs is low. The linear relationship of fluorescence intensity over concentration was enhanced with increasing aging, but the correlation was small (Fig. 4r). In addition, as the degree of aging increased, the fluorescence intensity tended to increase. Then, the effect of the UV aging process on the interfacial behavior was analyzed in relation to the functional group changes during the aging process of submicron microspheres. According to the above, the carbonyl group decreases and the hydroxyl group increases slightly after UV aging, but the value of Zeta Potential is more negative relative to NF aging, and the dispersed system is more stable compared to NF-aged submicron microspheres (Table S4).

The UV absorption of PS-AA with different aging treatments is shown in Fig. S7. The submicron microspheres have strong UV absorption at 232 nm. The absorbance of the submicron microspheres decreased with aging. NF aging had the greatest effect on UV absorption, followed by UV aging, while NH had the smallest effect. Whereas, the slight increase in fluorescence intensity of R-CDs due to UV aging treatment may be related to the decrease in absorbance of submicron plastics.

Moreover, the slopes of the UV absorption spectra decreased after NF and UV aging. While the slope decrease of NH is not obvious. It may be related to the change of functional groups for UV absorption. The UV absorption at 195, 230–250 nm comes from C-C. UV radiation at 300–400 nm can cause the polymer transform into excited states. The excited C-H bonds readily react with oxygen to form hydroperoxides, which then decompose into free radicals (Liu et al., 2023b). The UV absorption around 230 nm comes from the hydroxyl group. The overall weakening of the absorption peak is due to the aging of the submicron microspheres resulting in surface roughness and decreased light transmission. The overall scattering of light by the solution decreases, and the transmittance increases, resulting in a decrease in absorbance. The UV absorption capacity of NF-aged PS-AA was diminished, which was mainly affected by iron deposition on the MPs surface.

In addition, the hydrodynamic diameter increased after aging (Table S4), where the increase in NF aging was greater than the increase in UV aging. The increase in surface roughness and hydrophilic groups are the main factors leading to the increase in hydrodynamic diameter. In addition, according to Fig. 4q and r, the change in the slope of the fitted equations after aging has a certain linear relationship (0.91 and 0.66, respectively, Fig. S8). This could be closely related to the increased surface roughness, the rise in oxygen-containing functional groups, and the enhanced adsorption and deposition of Fe after aging. Based on the fluorescence response of aged MPs with R-CDs, it is helpful to evaluate the environmental risk and carrying contaminants behavior of MPs.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Changes in scattering intensity of PS-AA coexisting with B-CDs at different aging degrees
ce:section.ce:para: The effect of aging on the interaction of PS-AA with fluorescent CDs was further investigated using B-CDs. Relative to R-CDs, B-CDs have better hydrophilicity, lower Stokes displacement, and lower surface charge.

The excitation light enters the solution and is partly absorbed by the B-CDs and partly elastically scattered in the presence of PS-AA. Elastic scattering can be considered as an ejection collision between a photon and the scattering center, where the photon energy does not change, so the scattered light has the same wavelength as the incident light. By the action of radiation, the molecule and the interaction of the excitation light causes polarization, which can be regarded as a jump of electrons to the excited virtual state, and the electrons on the energy level of the virtual state quickly return to the lower energy level or the ground state and produce scattered light. The main factors affecting the intensity of scattering light are the wavelength of the light, the scale of the scattering center, and the surface properties of the particles. In addition to elastic scattering, inelastic scattering also occurs and is not considered because of its relatively weak intensity (Bickel et al., 1976). Whereas the scattered light is linearly polarized in the direction where the angle between the scattered light and the incident light is 90°, the degree of anisotropy of the scattered particles can be estimated using the polarization properties of the scattered light. Liu et al. (2024) based on the analysis of polarization state information of 120° scattered light, which enabled the analysis of the degree of weathering of MPs and polymer type.

In addition, the addition of B-CDs reduces the intensity of the scattering peaks, implying that the number of photons undergoing elastic scattering is reduced or the fluorescence of B-CDs is enhanced by partially scattered photons. Therefore, the interfacial interaction of microplastics with B-CDs was analyzed by the ratio of the number of photons scattered to the number of photons emitted by fluorescence (Figs. 5 , S9, and S10). The scattering/PL showed a good linear relationship with concentration changes, but the slope of the equation decreased with aging, and the change in slope may be a basis for assessing the environmental risk of MPs. It is noteworthy that the change in the slope of the fitted straight line after UV aging has a good linear relationship (0.97, Fig. S8), which may be attributed to the regular increase in the oxygen-containing functional groups on the surface with aging time. However, the lower correlation of the slope changes after NF aging that may be caused by a combination of factors such as surface roughness, Fe deposition, and so on (0.45, Fig. S8).

Gradually increasing the concentration of PS-AA in the B-CDs, scattering peaks and fluorescence peaks of the B-CDs can be observed. The scattering peaks increased regularly with increasing concentration, while the fluorescence peaks increased slightly and without a clear trend. The effect of aging on the scattering peaks of submicron microspheres was further investigated. According to the results of Fig. S11, the scattering peak decreases after aging under multiple conditions. The scattering peak linearly enhanced with increasing concentration. The intensity of the scattering peak decreases with the increase of aging, but the linear correlation increases. Therefore, direct detection of MPs based on the light scattering principle is affected by the degree of aging of MPs.

As indicated by the UV absorption analysis (Fig. S7), the aging treatments caused a decrease in the UV absorption of PS-AA, with the NF treatment decreasing the most, and the slope from 232–500 nm decreasing significantly. This is followed by the UV treatment, while the NH treatment induces UV absorption. It is worth noting that the deposition of Fe on the surface of PS-AA is an important factor leading to the decrease in UV absorption of NF-treated PS-AA. In addition, based on the changes in surface properties of PS-AA under different treatment conditions in Fig. 2, the properties of the NH-treated surfaces are basically the same as those of the original surfaces, with smooth and sharp surfaces. However, the surfaces treated with NF and UV are significantly rougher. The changes in UV absorption reflect the structural and functional group (chromophore and auxochrome) changes related to PS-AA under aging effects (Rabek et al., 1974).

Thus, changes in surface properties after weathering, such as disruption of the π-conjugate system, increase in surface roughness/surface area, and adsorption of contaminants lead to changes in the scattering peaks and UV absorption.

The core structure of the R-CDs is a Mg-porphyrin ring, which was constructed by replacing the center with a magnesium atom. Based on the relevant literature (Kasprzyk et al., 2018), the typical structure of B-CDs was constructed with 4-hydroxy-1H-pyrrolo[3,4-c] pyridine-1,3,6 (2H,5H)-trione. Since the construction of three-dimensional models of CDs is not clear in the existing literature, the fluorescence unit of CDs is used as a model to obtain partial information about the fluorescence and electric charge of CDs. The lowest energy structure was obtained by optimizing the structure at B3LYP/6–31G*. PS-AA performs DFT calculations by simulating the hydrophilic end of the copolymer and the elongated end of the chain during the polymerization of soap-free emulsions. As the outer layer of the microplastic is the first to be subjected to aging, the hydrophilic end of the copolymer was used as a model to study the aging behavior of PS-AA.

The interaction between CDs and PS-AA can be determined by the distribution of electrostatic potentials (Fig. 6 a-d). The side of the benzene ring has a more obvious negative charge, while the opposite side of the benzene ring has a more visible positive charge. Since the oxygen-containing functional groups of the copolymerized zwitterions of styrene and acrylic acid are hydrophilic they make up the outer side of PS-AA, while the inner side has a more positive charge. In contrast, the R-CDs structural unit planes have more negative charge aggregation, so the edges and centers of the structure exhibit positive charges. Thus, the planar structure of the R-CDs has some affinity for the main chain portion of PS-AA, while it is mainly repulsive for the hydrophilic end of PS-AA. However, B-CDs have a larger positive charge and more affinity with the hydrophilic side of PS-AA. This property is consistent with the zeta potential (Table S5). The fluorescent units of R-CDs and B-CDs were further combined with the PS-AA construct system to simulate the interfacial interactions of CDs with MPs, respectively (Fig. S12). It can be observed that the electrostatic potentials of the two units in the system overlap, meaning that hydrogen bonds can be formed under certain conditions. However, PS-AA, R-CDs, and B-CDs all have negative surface charges and exhibit repulsive forces on each other. The repulsion between R-CDs and PS-AA is stronger.

In addition, the quenching mechanism of R-CDs was simulated based on DFT calculations (Fig. 6e). The Mg of the fluorescence unit of R-CDs was substituted under the combined effect of Fe and anions, resulting in the change of the original closed-shell structure to an open-shell structure. The energy level overlap property of the outer electrons of Fe atoms changes the paths and energy differences of the electron excitation transitions. Further, we compared the fluorescence lifetimes of R-CDs mixed with MPs (NF treatment), and found that the fluorescence lifetimes of R-CDs decreased from 1.13 to 0.74 ns (Figs. 1 and S1).

The HOMO-LUMO gap is the energy gap between the highest occupied orbital and the lowest unoccupied orbital, which is numerically approximated by the fundamental gap or optical gap, with some positive correlation. According to the results (Fig. S13), the R-CDs and B-CDs have a smaller HOMO-LUMO gap and thus are easily excited. And due to the stacked graphite carbon structure, it has excellent fluorescence emission, resulting in more active electron excitation and emission on the CDs. And B-CDs (4.20 eV) have a larger gap compared to R-CDs (2.98 eV), so that the energy released when electrons return to the ground state from the excited state is higher, and thus the fluorescence emission of B-CDs is a higher energy blue light.

Similarly, the change in HOMO-LUMO gap of PS-AA with the aging effect was analyzed based on DFT calculations (Fig. 6f-i). After aging, more carbonyls were formed on the MPs surface and the HOMO-LUMO gap decreased (6.16 to 4.53 eV). According to the relationship between wavelength and energy of light as well as the principle of energy level transition, the capacity of PS-AA to absorb low-wavelength and high-energy light becomes smaller. And this same change affects the virtual state energy levels during elastic scattering, leading to reduced scattering. In addition, the elastic scattering of MPs is influenced by other factors such as surface structure, roughness, and carrying contaminants. Meanwhile, we compare the HOMO-LUMO gap after PS and PS-AA after aging (Fig. S14), and notice that the change trends are the same and the energies are similar, so it is not differentiated for discussion.

Furthermore, the two polystyrene units were calculated using the Fukui function (Fig. 6 and S15). The C of the benzene ring towards the outside is more vulnerable to attack and the carbon at the edge of the chain is more susceptible to attack. Qiu et al. (2024) pointed out that ROS are more inclined to attack the main chain of PE, while the C=C π bond on the benzene ring of PS is more easily attacked. The oxidation of PS-AA by hydroxyl radicals was simulated (Fig. S16) by constructing the initial structure combined with the results of the Fukui function. The hydroxyl radical replaces the H on the outer benzene ring first and forms a water molecule. After that, the C of the benzene ring linked to the main chain on the PS-AA copolymerization segment is more easily attacked. And the neighboring and opposite C on the elongated end of the chain are more easily attacked. In contrast, the C-C bond and C-H bond on the main chain are more stable. The C on the benzene ring is oxidized and ring opening eventually occurs by generating -CHO. By calculating the Fukui function in a step-by-step method, a consistent conclusion is obtained (Fig. S17). This also explains the changes in the interfacial behavior of PS-AA after aging, such as rougher morphology, increased adsorption capacity, decreased absorbance, and decreased scattering.

In this study, we investigated the fluorescence response and scattering phenomena generated by MPs with CDs during aging based on the interfacial interaction between fluorescent CDs and MPs. It provides a reliable reference for studying the carrying contaminants on MPs and optical detection of MP using fluorescent CDs.

With increasing of aging time, the fluorescence response of MPs with R-CDs increases, and its linear relationship with concentration is helpful for the quantitative analysis of MPs. The aging treatment also reduces the scattering phenomenon of MPs, which is due to the increase of oxygen-containing functional groups and the opening of the benzene ring, which leads to the decrease of the optical properties, resulting in a reduction of the scattering peaks. The combined analysis of fluorescence response and scattering data helped to further characterize the changes in microplastic interface properties and carrier contamination as a result of aging (Fig. S18). Fluorescence response and light scattering behavior based on CDs can improve our understanding of the environmental fate of MPs and contribute to the development of rapid MPs detection techniques.

Chao Liu: Writing – original draft, Visualization, Investigation, Formal analysis, Data curation. Yuan Jiao: Writing – original draft, Supervision, Methodology, Formal analysis, Conceptualization. Chunfan Yang: Data curation. Bo Li: Validation, Methodology. Wenjun Li: Validation, Resources. Tianwei Qian: Writing – review & editing, Supervision, Funding acquisition, Conceptualization. Xiaona Liu: Writing – review & editing, Supervision, Conceptualization.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This work was financially supported by the Fundamental Research Program of Shanxi Province (No. 202303021221041), the Science and Technology Innovation Project of colleges and universities in Shanxi Province (2021L029), the National construction of high-level university public graduate project (CSC202406930025), and the Special Program for Doctoral Students under the Young Talent Support Project of the Chinese Association for Science and Technology.

ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary material associated with this article can be found, in the online version, at doi:10.1016/j.watres.2025.123377.
